conclusion: Microsoft Volume I traces Bill Gates and Paul Allen from Lakeside School PDP-10 access (1968) to the IBM PC deal that created modern software economics. Born 1955 alongside the microprocessor wave, they wrote Altair BASIC without a finished product, licensed to MITS ($180K lifetime cap — mistake), won 1977 arbitration, then priced BASIC at ~$30 to every OEM to kill rivals — Apple paid $31,000. IBM Project Chess (1980) needed DOS; Gary Kildall missed the meeting flying his plane; Microsoft bought QDOS (~$50K–$75K), licensed non-exclusively to IBM while retaining OEM freedom — transferring IBM's dominance into Microsoft's. Mary Gates' board network and Bill Sr.'s law firm shaped negotiations young Bill 'constantly underestimated' leveraged. Steve Ballmer joined 1980; Simonyi brought applications vision. Positive spiral: lower price, more adoption, more software, stronger standard. Part I ends pre-Windows dominance — splinter moment: IBM played checkers, Gates played 3D chess.
background: Ten years into Acquired, Ben and David tackle Microsoft Volume I (~49 years, $3T+ today per episode). Bill Gates III grows up in Seattle power circles (Mary Gates on UW Regents, national United Way; Bill Sr. co-founds Preston Gates → K&L Gates). Lakeside gets teletype to GE timeshare 1968; Paul Allen sees Moore's Law before the phrase exists reading 8080 article (~$200 chip). Traf-O-Data fails (~$20K revenue); Altair 8800 cover (Jan 1975) prompts fake-it-till-you-make-it BASIC on Harvard PDP-10 emulator; Paul demos in Albuquerque — second try saves meeting. Micro-Soft partnership 60/40→64/36 Bill-led. MITS exclusive deal caps lifetime BASIC at $180K; piracy Open Letter (1975). Japan via Kay Nishi cold call (~50% revenue international early). IBM Boca Raton Project Chess chooses Intel 8088, Microsoft languages, QDOS (~$50K–$75K) as PC-DOS — non-exclusive license lets Microsoft sell MS-DOS to all clones.
important_facts: April 4, 1975: Micro-Soft founded in Albuquerque after Popular Electronics Altair cover; Bill and Paul demoed BASIC on Harvard PDP-10 emulator without finished product — second try saved the MITS meeting. MITS exclusive deal capped lifetime BASIC revenue at $180,000; Bill Sr.'s arbitration clause helped win November 1977 release to OEM license broadly.

Bill priced 8080 BASIC ~$30 per machine to OEMs — Apple paid $31,000 for standard versus finishing Woz's incomplete BASIC. 1975 revenue $16,000 from $30-per-copy Altair sales; 1977 ended $381,000 after zero revenue first 11 months post-arbitration; 1978 $1.3M with 13 employees; 1979 $2.4M before Seattle move for UW recruiting.

IBM PC (1981): Project Chess team ~30 people in Boca Raton — smaller than Microsoft's deal team. Gary Kildall/Digital Research missed IBM meeting flying his plane; Microsoft bought QDOS from Seattle Computer Products (~$50K–$75K), customized PC-DOS, retained non-exclusive MS-DOS OEM rights — IBM marketed PC, Microsoft captured clone wave.

Altair sold ~$397 (~$2,300 in 2024 dollars) versus DEC mini-computer ~$120,000; Intel 8080 list price $360, Ed Roberts negotiated volume deal ~$75 per chip enabling affordable kits. Bill born 1955 same year as Steve Jobs — timing mattered as much as strategy; Lakeside teletype 1968; Paul saw Moore's Law before the phrase existed.

IBM antitrust 1968 cracked bundled mainframe software — created opening for independent software vendors. Partnership split 60/40 then 64/36 Bill-led; Kay Nishi cold-called for Japan partnership building ~50% international revenue early. Open Letter to Hobbyists (1975) argued software labor deserves pay before copyright protection clear (1980) — piracy on unbundled Altair BASIC threatened the model.

mental_model.name: Positive Spiral Platform Pricing
mental_model.components: Microsoft's early insight: software is zero marginal cost with high fixed R&D — price for adoption, not margin maximization, to become the standard. MITS mistake taught exclusive caps ($180K lifetime) and piracy risk when hardware OEM does not bundle payment. OEM per-machine ~$30 royalty aligns incentives. IBM deal non-exclusivity lets Microsoft capture clone wave (Compaq) IBM enabled. Languages (BASIC, Fortran, COBOL) plus DOS integrate point of industry control. International early (Kay Nishi Japan ~50% revenue) expands spiral. Bill's competitive drive plus Paul/Moore insight plus Mary/Bill Sr. legal-network access compound luck (Altair cover timing, Kildall miss). NCR, GE, Control Data approached 1976 with ~$100K deals each — arbitration unlock changed economics.
mental_model.application: Platform startups: underprice to become default if marginal cost near zero and switching costs rise with adoption — but avoid exclusive caps that trap you (MITS $180K lesson). Partner with giants (IBM) only if you retain rights to sell same product to their competitors. Bill priced 8080 BASIC ~$30 flat — Apple paid $31,000 for standard versus finishing Woz's unfinished BASIC. Legal protection for software (1980) lagged invention — business model innovation preceded IP regime.

competitive_advantage: Volume I Microsoft advantage is emerging DOS/language standard on IBM PC clone wave. First-mover on microcomputer BASIC with ruthless OEM pricing (~$30) made alternatives irrational — Apple paid $31,000 versus hiring to finish Woz's BASIC. MITS exclusive deal capped lifetime BASIC revenue at $180,000 until Bill Sr. arbitration (Nov 1977) freed OEM licensing; 1977 ended with $381K revenue after zero for first 11 months. IBM Project Chess (~30 people) needed 16-bit OS; Gary Kildall missed meeting flying his plane; Microsoft bought QDOS (~$50K–$75K), licensed non-exclusively — IBM marketed PC, Microsoft captured clone wave (Compaq). Altair $397 (~$2,300 today) versus DEC mini ~$120,000; Intel 8080 list $360, Ed Roberts negotiated ~$75. Paul saw Moore's Law; Kay Nishi built Japan to ~50% revenue early. NCR, GE, and Control Data approached 1976 with ~$100K licensing deals each once arbitration freed Microsoft. Weaknesses: IBM hardware dependency; no application suite dominance yet (Part II); piracy until bundling; Bill's abrasive culture. Competitors (Digital Research CP/M) had moments to block spiral — missed. Power summary: counter-position software-only, scale economies on R&D, switching costs once devs write for MS-DOS, network effects via file formats later, cornered resource DOS. Traf-O-Data made ~$20,000 — Bill insisted software-only North Star while Paul explored hardware. 1975 Open Letter to Hobbyists argued software labor deserves pay before 1980 copyright protection existed.

key_insights.question: Why is the IBM deal the splinter?
key_insights.answer: Project Chess needed 16-bit OS fast; Kildall missed meeting flying his plane; Microsoft bought QDOS cheap (~$50K–$75K), customized PC-DOS, retained MS-DOS OEM rights — IBM's brand created demand Microsoft captured on all clones including Compaq. Project Chess team ~30 people; irony: project named Chess while Gates played multi-level game. IBM shipped PC in a year, revolutionized industry, captured little software value — non-exclusive license transferred dominance.

key_insights.question: Why an open letter to hobbyists?
key_insights.answer: 1975 piracy on Altair because software not bundled with hardware — Bill's Open Letter argued labor deserves pay before copyright clear (1980). Exclusive MITS deal capped lifetime BASIC revenue at $180,000 — Ed Roberts had upper hand. Bill Sr. arbitration clause freed OEM model Nov 1977; Microsoft ended 1977 with $381K revenue after zero first 11 months. Per-machine ~$30 fee aligns payment with each sale.

key_insights.view: Price to starve rivals, not maximize.
key_insights.question: Why $30 BASIC to Apple?
key_insights.answer: Bill priced 8080 BASIC ~$30 flat to every OEM — positive spiral: cheap enough every OEM buys Microsoft standard; volume funds R&D lead. Apple paid $31,000 versus hiring to finish Woz's BASIC. Requires low overhead (Bill/Paul coding) and patience; deliberately underprices versus value-maximizing when marginal cost near zero and switching costs rise with installed base.

key_insights.question: Was Bill the smartest in the room?
key_insights.answer: Harvard Math 55 yielded Bill a B — peers one-in-a-million exist. Born 1955; Lakeside teletype 1968; Altair 1975 cover timing. Paul saw exponential chips in spring 1974 Electronics Magazine 8080 article (~$200). Kay Nishi doubled international to ~50% revenue early. Wave mattered as much as IQ — Bill shifted to applied math/applied computing when pure math had sharper peers.

key_insights.question: Why no Microsoft hardware?
key_insights.answer: Bill insisted microprocessor software North Star — Paul tempted by hardware (Traf-O-Data made ~$20,000). While IBM integrated mainframes, Microsoft licensed bits with no inventory risk. Clone OEMs fought hardware wars at Altair $397 versus DEC $120,000; Microsoft collected DOS toll on all — counter-position versus IBM's integrated model.

top_investment_implications.thesis: Volume I seeds still visible: platform toll-taking, enterprise trust, international DNA — today's Azure/OpenAI extend same positive-spiral logic; Part II/III cover applications and cloud moats built on DOS origin.

golden_quotes: "Selling software is the best business model of all time" — Ben/David frame; Microsoft invented the standalone software company.
"IBM was playing chess… Bill Gates was playing 3D chess" — on Project Chess irony and non-exclusive DOS rights.
"You want your ecosystem generating more revenue than you capture" — Bill Gates line on platform health (cited in episode playbook).

Lakeside School gets PDP-10 teletype access; meets Paul
Intel 8080 announced; Paul moves to Boston; Traf-O-Data era
Popular Electronics Altair cover; fake BASIC call to MITS
Micro-Soft founded Albuquerque; MITS exclusive deal signed
Open Letter to Hobbyists on piracy; approached by NCR, GE
Arbitration wins; freed to OEM license; $381K revenue year
Kay Nishi partnership; Japan ~50% revenue; move Seattle
Steve Ballmer joins; IBM Project Chess contacts Microsoft
IBM PC announced with Microsoft DOS and languages — clone era begins
